guides.guideDescription: Guidelines developed by the European Association of Science Editors (EASE) to improve clarity, completeness, and international readability of scientific manuscripts written in English.

analysis.item_question: Is the title unambiguous, specific, and reflective of the article content?
analysis.item_question_background: Vague or ambiguous titles impair discoverability and may mislead readers about the study scope, increasing citation distortion and misclassification in indexing databases.
analysis.comment: The title is specific, unambiguous, and closely reflects the manuscript’s focus on the interaction of age, driving experience, and gender in predicting dangerous driving behavior.
quotes: How Age, Driving Experience, and Gender Interact in Predicting Dangerous Driving Behavior

analysis.item_question: If relevant, does the title include study period, location, organism scientific name, or experimental design?
analysis.item_question_background: Omitting contextual identifiers such as location, species, or design reduces reproducibility and prevents accurate interpretation of study generalizability.
analysis.comment: The title does not include additional contextual identifiers such as study period, location, organism scientific name, or explicit study design. These details are not clearly necessary for this manuscript title, so this item is optional.
quotes: How Age, Driving Experience, and Gender Interact in Predicting Dangerous Driving Behavior

analysis.item_question: If human subjects of only one sex were included, is this specified in the title?
analysis.item_question_background: Failure to disclose sex-restricted sampling obscures biological applicability and may contribute to sex-based bias in interpretation and downstream clinical translation.
analysis.comment: This item does not apply because the study included participants of both sexes rather than only one sex.
quotes: The sample included 1,882 drivers (50.9% men)
The sample comprised 1 , 882 drivers ... with 957 men (50.9%).

analysis.item_question: Are all authors listed with affiliations, present correspondence address, and email addresses?
analysis.item_question_background: Incomplete author metadata prevents accountability, inhibits correspondence, and complicates institutional attribution and research integrity verification.
analysis.comment: All authors are listed with an affiliation, and the corresponding author’s postal-style institutional address and email are provided. However, email addresses are not provided for all authors.
quotes: María Ángeles Ramos - Moreno 1 & Alberto Megías - Robles 1* 1 Department of Basic Psychology. University of Málaga.
Corresponding author Alberto Megías Robles Department of Basic Psychology. University of Málaga Email: amegias@uma.es

analysis.item_question: Are ORCiD identifiers provided for authors (encouraged)?
analysis.item_question_background: Without persistent digital identifiers, author disambiguation becomes unreliable, leading to attribution errors in citation tracking systems.
analysis.comment: I could not locate supporting evidence in the manuscript content provided for this request that ORCiD identifiers were supplied for the authors.

analysis.item_question: Does the abstract contain background, objectives, methods, results, and conclusions?
analysis.item_question_background: Structured abstracts reduce selective outcome emphasis and improve indexing accuracy by ensuring balanced representation of study rationale, execution, and findings.
analysis.comment: The abstract includes background/rationale, objective, methods, results, and an interpretive concluding statement, but it is presented as a single unstructured paragraph rather than a clearly structured abstract with explicit element labels.
quotes: While increasing age has been consistently associated with a safer driving style , the role of driving experience and its interplay with age remain s insufficiently understood . Using a large community - based sample of drivers, this study aimed to clarify the relative, joint, and interactive contribution s of age, driving experience, and gender to the propensity to engage in dangerous driving behaviors . The frequency of such behaviors w as assessed using the Dula Dangerous Driving Index (DDDI). The sample included 1,882 drivers (50.9% men) with a mean age of 38.11 years (range 18 – 79), a mean driving experience of 15.94 years, and a mean annual mileage of 6,000 – 9,000 km.

Results indicated that increasing age was associated with a reduced propensity for dangerous driving behavior, but only after sufficient driv ing experience had been acquired... These findings emphasize the need for multifactorial approaches that account fo r the interaction s among contributing variables to guide the implementation of effective road - safety measures .

analysis.item_question: Does the abstract avoid references to tables or figures?
analysis.item_question_background: Abstracts are often indexed independently; referencing tables or figures creates inaccessible claims when separated from the full manuscript.
analysis.comment: The abstract does not refer to tables or figures.
analysis.quote_verification_note: This item is satisfied because the manuscript avoids prohibited wording, so there may be no supporting quote.

analysis.item_question: Does the abstract avoid literature references unless absolutely necessary and fully detailed?
analysis.item_question_background: Citations in abstracts can mislead indexing systems and create incomplete bibliographic records when abstracts are distributed without reference lists.
analysis.comment: The abstract avoids literature citations/references.
analysis.quote_verification_note: This item is satisfied because the manuscript avoids prohibited wording, so there may be no supporting quote.

analysis.item_question: Is all information presented in the abstract also reported in the main body?
analysis.item_question_background: Discrepancies between abstract and body may constitute selective reporting and distort reader interpretation or meta-analytic extraction.
analysis.comment: The key information in the abstract is reflected in the main body, including the study aim, sample characteristics, DDDI assessment, the age-by-experience pattern, stronger effects among men, and the concluding interpretation.
quotes: Using a large community - based sample of drivers, this study aimed to clarify the relative, joint, and interactive contribution s of age, driving experience, and gender to the propensity to engage in dangerous driving behaviors . The frequency of such behaviors w as assessed using the Dula Dangerous Driving Index (DDDI). The sample included 1,882 drivers (50.9% men) with a mean age of 38.11 years (range 18 – 79), a mean driving experience of 15.94 years

Building on the previous discussion, t he present study aimed to take a step further in understanding how age and driving experience are associated with dangerous driving behavior... To test these hypotheses, w e assessed drivers’ propensity for dangerous behavior using the Dula Dangerous Driving Index ( DDDI ; risky driving and aggressive driving subscales )... The sample comprised 1 , 882 drivers... analysis.item_question: Do keywords include relevant scientific terms not already in the title?
analysis.item_question_background: Redundant keywords reduce indexing breadth and may limit discoverability across thematic databases.
analysis.comment: The keyword list includes some relevant scientific terms not already in the title, such as 'Risk-taking behavior' and 'DDDI', but several keywords repeat title concepts ('Dangerous driving', 'Age', 'Driving experience', 'Gender differences'), reducing indexing breadth.

How Age, Driving Experience, and Gender Interact in Predicting Dangerous Driving Behavior

analysis.item_question: Are the keywords specific rather than general?
analysis.item_question_background: Overly broad keywords dilute search precision and impair targeted retrieval in bibliographic databases.
analysis.comment: The keywords are generally specific to the study topic and methods rather than overly broad. They identify the behavior studied, a related construct, key predictors, and the instrument used.
quotes: Keywords: Dangerous driving; Risk - taking behavior; Age; Driving experience; Gender differences; DDDI .

analysis.item_question: Are broader interdisciplinary terms included when appropriate?
analysis.item_question_background: Failure to include cross-disciplinary indexing terms can isolate research from adjacent fields where findings may have translational relevance.
analysis.comment: I could not locate supporting evidence that broader interdisciplinary indexing terms were included beyond the topic-specific keyword list.

guides.guideDescription: Guidance developed by the European Association of Science Editors (EASE) to improve clarity, transparency, ethical compliance, and research integrity in scientific manuscripts structured according to the IMRaD format.

analysis.item_question: Does the Introduction clearly explain why the study was needed?
analysis.item_question_background: Without a clearly stated rationale, readers cannot assess the scientific gap being addressed, which undermines the justification for the study and weakens its contribution to the field.
analysis.comment: The Introduction clearly justifies the study by describing the public health importance of dangerous driving, summarizing unresolved debate about age versus driving experience, and identifying the unclear nature of their interrelation.
quotes: A detailed understanding of how these factors influence driving behavior is crucial for designing effective prevention strategies and enhancing road safety. In this regard, although age and driving experience have previously been studied together as predictors of dange ro us driving ( Brown & Groeger, 1988; de Winter & Dodou, 2010 ; Huang & Winston, 2011; Maycock et al., 1991; McCartt et al., 2009 ) , the nature of their interrelation remains unclear .

Despite extensive research on the roles of age, experience, and gender, considerable debate persist s in the literature over the relative importance of these factors in promoting dangerous driving behaviors .

analysis.item_question: Are the research objectives or specific research questions clearly stated?
analysis.item_question_background: If research objectives are vague or absent, readers cannot determine what the study intends to answer, compromising interpretability and evaluation of results.
analysis.comment: The manuscript clearly states the study objectives near the end of the Introduction, including examination of interaction effects and gender moderation.
quotes: Building on the previous discussion, t he present study aimed to take a step further in understanding how age and driving experience are associated with dangerous driving behavior.

Rather than focusing solely on their individual associations or treating them as covariates to be controlled for, we sought to examine the possibility of an interaction between the se variables . This analysis was complemented by examining gender - related differences and their potential moderating effect s on the relationship between age, experience, and dangerous driving.

analysis.item_question: Does the Introduction move from general background to the specific research question?
analysis.item_question_background: A poorly structured introduction can obscure the logical development of the research problem, making it difficult to follow how the specific question emerges from broader context.
analysis.comment: The Introduction follows a logical progression from broad road-safety context, to prior evidence on age, experience, and gender, to the unresolved debate, and finally to the specific study aims and hypotheses.
quotes: Traffic crashes remain a major public health concern worldwide, with dangerous driving practices constituting a leading cause of crashes and fatalities (World Health Organization, 2023).

However, we believe a crucial aspect of this debate that is generally overlooked is the p o tential interaction effect of these variables. The lack of driving skills among novice drivers is common across all ages and m ay moderate age - related effects.

analysis.item_question: If applicable, is the tested hypothesis explicitly formulated?
analysis.item_question_background: Failure to state a hypothesis in hypothesis-driven research obscures the analytical framework and limits the reader’s ability to evaluate whether conclusions align with prespecified expectations.
analysis.comment: The introduction explicitly formulates the study hypotheses near its end, including three numbered hypotheses and a note that additional interaction effects will be explored.
quotes: We hypothesized the following: (H1) In terms of individual variables, younger drivers will exhibit more dangerous driving behavior s than older drivers; with respect to driving experience, however, no clear directional hypothesis can be formulated regarding its relationship with dangerous driving behavior , given the mixed findings reported in the literature.

Nevertheless , we propose that (H2) the level of driving experience may influence the relationship between age and dangerous behavior, as discussed above ; and (H3) men will exhibit more dangerous driving behavior s than women. The i nteraction effects involving gender , age , and driving experience will be explored.

analysis.item_question: Does the Methods section describe in sufficient detail how the study was carried out?
analysis.item_question_background: Insufficient methodological detail prevents reproducibility and limits the ability of other researchers to verify or replicate the findings.
analysis.comment: The Methods section provides substantial procedural detail on participants, recruitment, ethics, measures, variable construction, analytic phases, regression models, follow-up analyses, and software, sufficient to understand how the study was carried out.
quotes: 2. Method 2.1. Participants The sample comprised 1 , 882 drivers . All participants held a valid car driving license and were recruited within the context of larger studies... Participants were recruited through advertisements at the Universities of Málaga and Granada... Subsequently , they provided electronic informed consent. Ethical approval for this project was granted by the Research Ethics Committee of the University of Málaga ( approval number: 14 – 2019 - H ).

2.2. Procedure and Instruments Before completing the DDDI , participants provided sociodemographic information, including gender, age, the number of years they had held a driving license... annual car mileage was assessed using a 12 - category scale... The Dula Dangerous Driving Index (DDDI) is a self - report instrument... It comprises 28 items divided into three subscales... 2.3. Data analysis D escriptive statistics and bootstrapped Pearson correlations were computed... a series of multiple linear regression analyses were conducted... All analyses were conducted using SPSS version 22 (IBM Corporation, Armonk , NY, USA) and the PROCESS macro version 4.2 (Hayes, 2022) .

analysis.item_question: Are all factors that could have affected the results considered?
analysis.item_question_background: Unreported confounding or influencing factors can introduce bias and mislead interpretation of the study’s findings.
analysis.comment: The manuscript considers several factors that could affect results, including gender, age, years licensed, annual mileage, interaction terms, and assumption checks, but it does not demonstrate that all plausible influencing factors were addressed in the study design or analysis.
quotes: In the first phase ( full sample), the predictors were Gender, Age, Years holding a driving license, Annual car mileage, the two - way interaction terms Age × Years holding a driving license and Age × Annual car mileage, as well as all corresponding interaction terms involving gender

Future research should aim to extend the present findings while addressing certain limitations of the current study. A primary concern is that the evaluation of driving behavior and experience relied on self - report measures , which are susceptible to social desirability bias and inaccura cies in individuals’ perceptions of their own behavior.

analysis.item_question: If experimental materials were obtained from biobanks, are full names and identifiers reported?
analysis.item_question_background: Failure to report biobank sources and identifiers impedes traceability of materials and may compromise reproducibility and ethical oversight.
analysis.comment: I could not locate supporting evidence in the manuscript content provided for this request that experimental materials were obtained from biobanks or that biobank names/identifiers were relevant.

analysis.item_question: If citing non-English or inaccessible methods, are they fully explained?
analysis.item_question_background: Relying on inaccessible sources without detailed explanation prevents verification and limits accessibility for international readers.
analysis.comment: The Methods section does not rely on a non-English or otherwise inaccessible method citation without explanation; when prior procedures are referenced, the manuscript provides the operative details directly in the text.
quotes: Following the procedure used by Megías - Robles et al. (2022) and Megías et al. (2018), annual car mileage was assessed using a 12 - category scale: 1 = “Nothing,” 2 = “Less than 1,000 km,” 3 = “1,000 – 3,000 km,” 4 = “3,000 – 6,000 km,” 5 = “6,000 – 9,000 km,” 6 = “9,000 – 12,000 km,” 7 = “12,000 – 15,000 km,” 8 = “15,000 – 18,000 km,” 9 = “18,000 – 21,000 km,” 10 = “21,000 – 30,000 km,” 11 = “30,000 – 40,000 km,” and 12 = “More than 40,000 km.”

The Dula Dangerous Driving Index (DDDI) is a self - report instrument employed to measure dangerous driving behavior (Dula & Ballard, 2003; Sánchez - López et al., 2024; Willemsen et al., 2008). It comprises 28 items divided into three subscales: Risky Driving (DDDI - RD; 12 items), Aggressive Driving (DDDI - AD; 7 items), and Negative Cognitive/Emotional Driving (DDDI - NCED, formerly referred to as NED in some prior studies; 9 items). Participants are asked to indicate the frequency with which they engage in specific driving behaviors, responding to each item on a 5 - point Likert scale (1 = ‘Never’, 2 = ‘Rarely’, 3 = ‘Sometimes’, 4 = ‘Often’, 5 = ‘Always’).

analysis.item_question: Does the manuscript confirm compliance with relevant ethical standards?
analysis.item_question_background: Failure to report ethical compliance raises concerns about participant protection, animal welfare, or environmental harm, potentially invalidating the study.
analysis.comment: The Methods section confirms ethical compliance, including adherence to the Declaration of Helsinki, informed consent, and institutional ethics approval with approval number.
quotes: The study was conducted in accordance with the ethical principles of the Declaration of Helsinki (World Medical Association, 2013). Prior to participation, individuals were informed about the study procedures, the voluntary nature of their involvement, and the anonymity of their data. Subsequently, they provided electronic informed consent.
Ethical approval for this project was granted by the Research Ethics Committee of the University of Málaga (approval number: 14–2019-H).

analysis.item_question: Does the Results section present only new findings from the study?
analysis.item_question_background: Including previously published data in the Results section can confuse the originality of findings and distort the distinction between background and novel contributions.
analysis.comment: The Results section mainly reports this study's findings, but it also includes interpretive and explanatory material that is more appropriate for the Discussion, so it is not limited strictly to new findings.
quotes: Results indicated that increasing age was associated with a reduced propensity for dangerous driving behavior, but only after sufficient driv ing experience had been acquired.

Finally, to ensure consistency with the analysis of the full sample, an additional analysis was conducted in the reduced subsample using years holding a driving license and annual car mileage as predictors. This analysis is provided in the supplementary ma terial (see Section 2). Results were similar to those obtained in the full sample, with annual car mileage emerging as the relevant factor within driving experience.

analysis.item_name: Tables and Figures Referenced and Ordered
analysis.item_question: Are all tables and figures mentioned in the text and numbered sequentially?
analysis.item_question_background: Improperly referenced or misnumbered tables and figures reduce clarity and hinder the reader’s ability to verify reported findings.
analysis.comment: All tables and the figure are cited in the text, and numbering appears sequentially from Table 1 through Table 6 and Figure 1.
quotes: Descriptive statistics for the full sample of drivers are presented in Table 1. Bootstrapped Pearson correlations (5,000 resamples) among the DDDI scores and drivers’ sociodemographic characteristics are presented in Table 2.

Figure 1. S catter plots illustrating the significant interactions from the multiple regression models predicting DDDI scores, with points representing observed values and regression lines representing predicted values. Panel A shows the Gender × Age × Annual car mileage interaction for the DDDI - RD subscales... Panel B shows the Age × Annual car mileage interaction for the DDDI - AD subscale.

analysis.item_question: Is the statistical analysis appropriate and correctly applied?
analysis.item_question_background: Inappropriate statistical methods can lead to incorrect conclusions and invalidate the study’s findings.
analysis.comment: The statistical analysis appears appropriate to the study design and variables: descriptive statistics, bootstrapped correlations, and multiple linear regression with interaction terms are aligned with the stated hypotheses and the noted normality violation.
quotes: Given the violation of the normality assumption , subsequent analyses were conducted using a bias - corrected bootstrapping procedure (5,000 resamples) to obtain robust parameter estimat es . Regression coefficients were interpreted based on 95% bootstrap confidence intervals, with intervals not including zero indicating a significant relationship.

Next, in accordance with our hypotheses, a series of multiple linear regression analyses were conducted, with each DDDI subscale serving as the criterion variable . In the first phase ( full sample), the predictors were Gender, Age, Years holding a driving license, Annual car mileage, the two - way interaction terms Age × Years holding a driving license and Age × Annual car mileage, as well as all corresponding interaction terms involving gender.

analysis.item_question: Are data disaggregated by sex when involving humans or animals?
analysis.item_question_background: Aggregating data without sex disaggregation can obscure biologically meaningful differences and limit the generalizability of findings.
analysis.comment: The Results section reports data separately by sex, including sex-stratified descriptive statistics and sex-specific interaction findings.
quotes: Table 1. Means and standard deviations of the study variables in the full sample, for all participants and by gender.

Follow-up analyses of the second-order interaction revealed that, among men, Age was negatively related to DDDI-RD scores at medium ... and high levels of annual car mileage ... For women, Age was negatively related to DDDI-RD regardless of Annual car mileage

analysis.item_question: Is there evidence that data are complete, not fabricated, distorted, or selectively excluded?
analysis.item_question_background: Data fabrication, selective omission, or image manipulation constitute scientific misconduct and fundamentally compromise research integrity.
analysis.comment: The manuscript reports sample sizes, descriptive statistics, correlations, regression models, supplementary analyses, and notes one omitted subscale from the main analyses with results summarized and referred to supplementary material. This provides some transparency and internal consistency between methods and results, but the manuscript does not provide direct evidence establishing that the data are complete, not fabricated, distorted, or selectively excluded.
quotes: The sample comprised 1 , 882 drivers .

The analyses focused on the DDDI - RD and DDDI - AD subscales . The DDDI - NCED subscale w as not considered, as it do not directly target intentional dangerous driving behaviors. However, for readers who may be interested, the main findings for th is subscale are briefly summarized at the end of the Results section, and the full results are reported in the supplementa ry material (see Section 1 ).

analysis.item_question: Does the Discussion avoid introducing new results?
analysis.item_question_background: Introducing new data in the Discussion blurs structural boundaries and prevents proper scrutiny of results presentation.
analysis.comment: The Discussion interprets findings already presented in Results and does not appear to introduce new statistical results for the first time.
quotes: The results of our study support the role of age as a predictor of dangerous driving behaviors. In line with our hypothesis (H1), age was negatively associated with DDDI - RD and DDDI - AD scores

We hypothesized (H2) that driving experience could moderate age - related effects on driving behavior . The multiple regression analyses revealed a significant interaction between Age and Annual car mileage in predicting DDDI - RD and DDDI - AD

analysis.item_question: Does the Discussion explicitly answer the research questions?
analysis.item_question_background: Failing to explicitly connect findings to the stated research questions weakens the logical coherence of the manuscript.
analysis.comment: The Discussion explicitly returns to the stated hypotheses/research questions and states whether the findings supported them.
quotes: The results of our study support the role of age as a predictor of dangerous driving behaviors. In line with our hypothesis (H1), age was negatively associated with DDDI - RD and DDDI - AD scores

We hypothesized (H2) that driving experience could moderate age - related effects on driving behavior ... Therefore, consistent with our second hypothesis, the results indicated that the previously discussed predictive roles of age and driving experience on dangerous driving were not present under all conditions.

analysis.item_question: Are the main results objectively compared with published data?
analysis.item_question_background: Without contextual comparison, readers cannot evaluate how the findings align with or diverge from existing evidence.
analysis.comment: The Discussion compares the main findings with prior literature using citations and interpretive comparison.
quotes: In line with our hypothesis (H1), age was negatively associated with DDDI - RD and DDDI - AD scores , suggesting that greater maturity contributes to a reduction in risky and aggressive driving behaviors ( de Winter & Dodou, 2010; Hatfield & Fernandes, 2009; Jonah, 1990; Megías - Robles et al., 2022; Navon – Eyal & Taubman – Ben - Ari, 2020; Rhodes & Pivik , 2011 ).

These results are consistent with a strand of the existing literature suggesting that, as drivers gain experience, their increased perception of self - efficacy may foster riskier and aggressive driving attitudes and a higher frequency of traffic violations, driven by greater confidence in the skills acquired over time ( Catchpole et al., 2005; de Winter and Dodou, 2010; Jing et al., 2023; Machado - León et al., 2016 ; Qu et al., 2014 ).

analysis.item_question: Are study limitations discussed?
analysis.item_question_background: Failure to disclose limitations may overstate confidence in findings and mislead readers regarding their reliability.
analysis.comment: The Discussion explicitly acknowledges several study limitations, including reliance on self-report measures, potential validity issues in using annual mileage as accumulated experience, possible exposure effects, sample distribution constraints, and the need for more older recent-license drivers and longitudinal designs.
quotes: Future research should aim to extend the present findings while addressing certain limitations of the current study. A primary concern is that the evaluation of driving behavior and experience relied on self - report measures , which are susceptible to social desirability bias and inaccura cies in individuals’ perceptions of their own behavior.

A nnual car mileage may fluctuate over time, particularly among older drivers , potentially affecting its validity as a measure of accumulated experience. A related consideration is that using mileage as an indicator of driving experience may introduce an exposure effect (Brown, 1982; Clarke et al., 2006), since greater distances travelled inherently increase the likelihood of engaging in dangerous behaviors .

analysis.item_question: Are the main findings clearly highlighted?
analysis.item_question_background: If key findings are not emphasized, the study’s primary contribution may be obscured.
analysis.comment: The Discussion clearly highlights the main findings by summarizing the effects of age, annual mileage/experience, gender, and especially the age-by-experience interaction on dangerous driving behaviors.
quotes: The results of our study support th e role of age as a predictor of dangerous driving behaviors. In line with our hypothesis (H1), age was negatively associated with DDDI - RD and DDDI - AD scores , suggesting that greater maturity contributes to a reduction in risky and aggressive driving behaviors

Specifically, h igher age was associated with a lower tendency to engage in risky and aggressive behaviors only among drivers with sufficient experience. Among less experienced drivers, the frequency of such behaviors d id not vary as a function of age. Furthermore, greater driving experience w as associated with higher levels of dangerous behavior only among younger and middle - aged groups.

analysis.item_question: If the study included only one sex, are implications for generalizability discussed?
analysis.item_question_background: Failing to discuss single-sex sampling limits transparency regarding external validity across sexes.
analysis.comment: This item does not apply because the study did not include only one sex; the sample included both men and women and analyzed gender differences.
quotes: The sample included 1,882 drivers (50.9% men) with a mean age of 38.11 years (range 18 – 79)

analysis.item_question: Are findings contrary to the authors’ viewpoint considered?
analysis.item_question_background: Ignoring contradictory evidence introduces confirmation bias and reduces the credibility of interpretations.
analysis.comment: The discussion considers findings and interpretations that differ from a simple age-only or experience-only viewpoint, and it acknowledges mixed prior evidence regarding driving experience.
quotes: While increasing age is consistently associated with more cautious driving behavior, the impact of driving experience — and its relationship with age — remains inconclusive, with studies reporting mixed findings

Regarding driving experience, our findings indicated that annual car mileage was a more relevant indicator than the number of years holding a driving license. Annual car mileage was positively associated with the two assessed measures of dangerous driving behaviors... In contrast, the number of years holding a driving license was not significantly associated with any of these subscales.

analysis.item_question: Are major conclusions and practical significance clearly emphasized?
analysis.item_question_background: Without a clear conclusion and statement of practical relevance, readers may struggle to understand the study’s real-world or theoretical impact.
analysis.comment: The manuscript clearly emphasizes the main conclusions and practical significance in the conclusion, including the interaction between age and experience and its implications for understanding dangerous driving and road-safety measures.
quotes: our findings indicate that age and driving experience do not operate independently; rather, they interact in shaping dangerous driving tendencies.
These findings emphasize the need for multifactorial approaches that account for the interactions among contributing variables to guide the implementation of effective road-safety measures .

title.label: Title page
title.text:
How Age, Driving Experience, and Gender Interact in Predicting
Dangerous Driving Behavior
María Ángeles Ramos - Moreno 1 & Alberto Megías - Robles 1*
1 Department of Basic Psychology. University of Málaga.
Corresponding author
Alberto Megías Robles
Department of Basic Psychology.
University of Málaga
Email: amegias@uma.es
title.startQuote: How Age, Driving Experience, and Gender Interact
quotes: How Age, Driving Experience, and Gender Interact
Email: amegias@uma.es

entries.affiliation: Department of Basic Psychology. University of Málaga.

entries.name: Alberto Megías-Robles
entries.affiliation: Department of Basic Psychology. University of Málaga.

abstract.label: Abstract
abstract.text:
While increasing age has been consistently associated with a safer driving style , the
role of driving experience and its interplay with age remain s insufficiently understood . Using
a large community - based sample of drivers, this study aimed to clarify the relative, joint, and
interactive contribution s of age, driving experience, and gender to the propensity to engage
in dangerous driving behaviors . The frequency of such behaviors w as assessed using the Dula
Dangerous Driving Index (DDDI). The sample included 1,882 drivers (50.9% men) with a
mean age of 38.11 years (range 18 – 79), a mean driving experience of 15.94 years, and a
mean annual mileage of 6,000 – 9,000 km. Results indicated that increasing age was
associated with a reduced propensity for dangerous driving behavior, but only after sufficient
driv ing experience had been acquired. Furthermore, greater driving experience predicted
higher dangerous driving behavior in younger and middle - aged drivers, while remaining
unrelated in older drivers. Notably, this pattern was more pronounced among men than
women. Th e observed interaction may be explained by the fact that novice drivers, regardless
of age, possess lower driving skills due to their limited experience, which may encourage a
more cautious driving style, offsetting the greater risk - taking typicall y observed in youth.
However, once sufficient experience is gained, younger drivers demonstrate a marked
tendency toward greater risk - taking behavior. These findings emphasize the need for
multifactorial approaches that account fo r the interaction s among contributing variables to
guide the implementation of effective road - safety measures .
abstract.endQuote: Keywords: Dangerous driving; Risk-taking behavior; Age;
quotes: Abstract
Keywords: Dangerous driving; Risk-taking behavior; Age;

introduction.label: 1. Introduction
introduction.text:
1. Introduction
Traffic crashes remain a major public health concern worldwide, with dangerous
driving practices constituting a leading cause of crashes and fatalities (World Health
Organization, 2023). Young drivers, particularly young men, account for a high percentage
of individuals involved in such risk behaviors and crashes ( Cassarino & Murphy, 2018; de
Winter & Dodou, 2010 ; Fergusson et al., 2003; McCartt, 2009 ). This overrepresentation is
often attributed to a combination of the higher risk propensity characteristic of youth
(Hatf ield & Fernandes, 2009; Jonah, 1986; Voogt et al. , 2014 ), together with limited driving
skills resulting from insufficient experience, as obtaining a driver’s license usually coincides
with this stage of life (Bjørnskau & Sagberg, 2005; Deery, 1999; Lee et al., 2008; McCartt et
al., 2009; McKenna et al., 2006) . A detailed understanding of how these factors influence
driving behavior is crucial for designing effective prevention strategies and enhancing road
safety. In this regard, although age and driving experience have previously been studied
together as predictors of dange ro us driving ( Brown & Groeger, 1988; de Winter & Dodou,
2010 ; Huang & Winston, 2011; Maycock et al., 1991; McCartt et al., 2009 ) , the nature of
their interrelation remains unclear .
The literature has consistently reported that the propensity to take risks behind the
wheel decreases with age, a pattern that is particularly evident when comparing drivers aged
18 – 24 with older drivers ( de Winter & Dodou, 2010 ; Hatfield & Fernandes, 2009; Jonah,
1990; Rhodes & Pivik , 2011; Sánchez‐López et al. , 2024; Voogt et al. , 2014 ; Yousaf et al.,
2024 ) . Th e heightened tendency to engage in dangerous driving behavior during youth has
been associated with elevated levels of impulsivity, greater risk propensity and sensation
seeking , perceived invulnerability, optimism bias, difficulties in emotion regulation, and
susceptibility to peer influence ( Aluja et al., 2023; DeJoy, 1992; Gheorghiu et al., 2015;
Hatfield et al. 2017; Huang & Winston, 2011 ; Ramos - Moreno et al. , 2025 ; Pizzo et al., 2024 ;
Rhodes et al., 2015; Starkey & Isler, 2016 ).
Neurodevelopmental theories further support this perspective , proposing that
adolescence and early adulthood are characterized by increased novelty - and sensation -
seeking behaviors, without a parallel maturation of the self - regulatory capacities needed to
manage these impulses effectively (Steinberg, 2008; 2010 ) . T his developmental imbalance

may help explain the peak in risky , and sometimes antisocial , driving behaviors observed
among young drivers. Empirical data on traffic crash rates support this relationship, show ing
a marked decrease in crash frequency among drivers aged 35+ compared with those aged
20 – 24 . Furthermore, although the most substantial decline occurs in the early stages of
adulthood , the downward trend in crash rates continues across older age groups (McCartt et
al., 2009; National Center for Statistics and Analysis, 2025). Taken together, t hese findings
highlight the critical influence of age - related factors on driving safety .
D riving experience , in turn, contributes to the development of critical skills such as
vehicle control, visual scanning, and hazard and risk perception , which improve
progressively through real - world exposure and training ( Borowsky et al., 2010; Deery, 1999 ;
Ge et al., 2017; McKnight & McKnight, 2003; Megías et al., 2018 ; Lehtonen et al. 2014 ).
Regardless of the driver's age, inexperience is associated with an increased risk of crashes
due to the insufficient development of these critical driving skills. Evidence indicates that
both young and older novice drivers initially exhibit high crash rates, which decline markedly
with practice, a reduction unlikely to be explained solely by maturity or other personal factors
( Huang & Winston, 2011; Langford et al. , 2006 ; Maycock et al., 1991; McCartt et al., 2009) .
Some studies even suggest that driving experience may be a more decisive factor than age
itself in preventing road crashes (McCartt et al., 2009 ; McKnight & McKnight, 2003 ) .
As outlined above, the link between driving experience and crash probability appears
to be well established; however, the relationship between driving experience and engagement
in dangerous driving behaviors — the p rimary focus of the present research — remains less
clear, with studies yielding mixed findings. On the one hand, the literature indicates that
inexperienced drivers are more susceptible to driving risks than their more experienced
counterparts, due to a limited ability to effectively detect and anticipate potential hazards . A s
the se drivers are still in a learning phase , develo ping driving skills and reducing errors are
ongoing processes ( Deery, 1999; McKenna et al., 2006; Lee et al., 2008). Consistent with
this view, Waller et al. (2001), in a study involving 13,809 novice drivers in Michigan, found
that the incidence of serious traffic violations declined with increasing driving experience,
with an ap proximate 8% reduction for each year of licensure during the initial years of
driving. On the other hand, as drivers become more experienced, their greater self - efficacy

may lead to riskier attitudes and a higher incidence of traffic violations due to increased
confidence in their driving skills . T hus, although experience can reduce certain risks by
enhancing skills, it may simultaneously promote more risk - taking behavior ( Catchpole et al.,
2005; de Winter & Dodou, 2010 ; Jing et al., 2023; Machado - León et al., 2016 ; Qu et al.,
2014 ). Supporting this notion, de Winter and Dodou’s (2010) meta - analysis reported that
driving violations are positively associated with driving experience. Evidence from several
longitudinal studies has also shown that, during the early stages of a driving career, driving
style tends to become riskier with increasing experience, measured as time since obtaining a
driving license, regardless of age or gender (Rowe et al., 2013; Wells et al., 2008).
Gender constitutes another important factor to consider in this context . Numerous
studies indicate that, compared with women, men are generally more prone to taking risks
on the road, engaging in aggressive and impulsive behaviors, committing traffic violations,
and incurring higher numbers of fines and crashes. In contrast, wo men tend to adopt more
responsible driving attitudes, which act as protective factors against dangerous driving
behaviors ( Cordellieri et al., 2016; de Winter & Dodou, 2010; González - Iglesias et al., 2012;
Megías - Robles et al., 2022; Navas et al., 2019; Ol tedal & Rundmo, 2006).
Despite extensive research on the roles of age, experience, and gender, considerable
debate persist s in the literature over the relative importance of these factors in promoting
dangerous driving behaviors . Some authors argue that developmental and psychological
characteristics associated with age are the most influential factors ( Harbeck et al., 2017;
Laapotti et al. , 2001; Ventsislavova et al., 2021), whereas others contend that the
accumulation of driving experience represents the primary determinant , regardless of a
driver’s chronological age ( Ge et al., 2017; Huang & Winston, 2011; Rowe et al., 2013;
Waller et al. , 2001 ). However, we believe a crucial aspect of this debate that is generally
overlooked is the p o tential interaction effect of these variables. The lack of driving skills
among novice drivers is common across all ages and m ay moderate age - related effects.
L ower proficiency, together with the resulting reduced sense of self - efficacy, may lead to
more cautious driving, potentially counteracting the higher risk - taking behavior that typically
characterizes younger drivers.

methods.label: 2. Method
methods.text:
2. Method
2.1. Participants
The sample comprised 1 , 882 drivers . All participants held a valid car driving license
and were recruited within the context of larger studies. T he mean age was 38.11 years (SD =
14.3 7 ; range = 18 – 79), with 957 men (50.9%). Participants had held their driving license for
an average of 15.94 years (SD = 13.48; range = 0 – 61) and reported a mean annual mileage
score of 5.45 (SD = 2.8 0 ), corresponding to an estimated driving distance of approximately
6,000 – 9,000 km per year according to the scale used in the study (see Procedure section).
Participants were recruited through advertisements at the Universities of Málaga and
Granada, with undergraduate students from these institutions assisting in the recruitment
process to ensur e a balanced sample across age and gender. The study was conducted in
accordance with the ethical principles of the Declaration of Helsinki (World Medical
Association, 2013). Prior to participation, individuals were informed about the study
procedure s , the voluntary nature of their involvement, and the anonymity of thei r data .
S ubsequently , they provided electronic informed consent. Ethical approval for this project
was granted by the Research Ethics Committee of the University of Málaga ( approval
number: 14 – 2019 - H ).
2.2. Procedure and Instruments
Before completing the DDDI , participants provided sociodemographic information,
including gender, age, the number of years they had held a driving license (hereafter referred
to as years holding a driving license), and annual car mileage. Following the procedure used
by Megías - Robles et al. (2022) and Megías et al. (2018), annual car mileage was assessed
using a 12 - category scale: 1 = “Nothing,” 2 = “Less than 1,000 km,” 3 = “1,000 – 3,000 km,”
4 = “3,000 – 6,000 km,” 5 = “6,000 – 9,000 km,” 6 = “9,000 – 12,000 km,” 7 = “12,000 – 15,000
km,” 8 = “15,000 – 18,000 km,” 9 = “18,000 – 21,000 km,” 10 = “21,000 – 30,000 km,” 11 =
“30,000 – 40,000 km,” and 12 = “More than 40,000 km.”
The Dula Dangerous Driving Index (DDDI) is a self - report instrument employed to
measure dangerous driving behavior (Dula & Ballard, 2003; Sánchez - López et al., 2024;
Willemsen et al., 2008). It comprises 28 items divided into three subscales: Risky Driving

(DDDI - RD; 12 items), Aggressive Driving (DDDI - AD; 7 items), and Negative
Cognitive/Emotional Driving (DDDI - NCED, formerly referred to as NED in some prior
studies; 9 items). Participants are asked to indicate the frequency with which they engage in
specific driving behaviors, responding to each item on a 5 - point Likert scale (1 = ‘Never’, 2
= ‘Rarely’, 3 = ‘Sometimes’, 4 = ‘Often’, 5 = ‘Always’). For the computation of mean scores
for each subscale, values were rescaled to a 0 – 4 range to facilitate interpre tation. The Spanish
version of the DDDI has demonstrated good internal consistency (Cronbach’s α = .8 6 for
DDDI - RD, .8 2 for DDDI - AD, and . 82 for DDDI - NCED; Sánchez - López et al., 2024). In the
present sample, internal consistency was acceptable, with Cronbach’s α = . 82 for DDDI - RD,
.78 for DDDI - AD, and .7 9 for DDDI - NCED.
2.3. Data analysis
The analyses focused on the DDDI - RD and DDDI - AD subscales . The DDDI - NCED
subscale w as not considered, as it do not directly target intentional dangerous driving
behaviors. However, for readers who may be interested, the main findings for th is subscale
are briefly summarized at the end of the Results section, and the full results are reported in
the supplementa ry material (see Section 1 ).
Prior to conducting analyses, the assumptions of normality and homoscedasticity of
the residuals were evaluated for the multiple regression models to ensure their robustness.
Visual inspection of Q – Q plots of the standardized residuals for the DDDI subscales revealed
deviations from normality . This observation was supported by significant Kolmogorov -
Smirnov and Shapiro - Wilk tests ( p < .001) . S kewness values rang ed from 0. 82 to 1. 15 and
excess kurtosis values rang ed from 0. 73 to 1 . 53 . Regarding homoscedasticity, inspection of
scatterplot s of standardized residuals against predicted values showed no evidence of
heteroscedasticity . Given the violation of the normality assumption , subsequent analyses
were conducted using a bias - corrected bootstrapping procedure (5,000 resamples) to obtain
robust parameter estimat es . Regression coefficients were interpreted based on 95% bootstrap
confidence intervals, with intervals not including zero indicating a significant relationship.
As explained in the Introduction, the data analysis was conducted in two phases . T he
first phase included th e full sample of participants, whereas the second phase focused on a

reduced subsample comprising only drivers who had held a driving license for 10 years or
less.
D escriptive statistics and bootstrapped Pearson correlations were computed for the
study variables in both phases . Next, in accordance with our hypotheses, a series of multiple
linear regression analyses were conducted, with each DDDI subscale serving as the criterion
variable . In the first phase ( full sample), the predictors were Gender, Age, Years holding a
driving license, Annual car mileage, the two - way interaction terms Age × Years holding a
driving license and Age × Annual car mileage, as well as all corresponding interaction terms
involving gender (Gender × Age, Gender × Years holding a driving license , Gender × Annual
car mileage, Gender × Age × Years holding a driving license , and Gender × Age × Annual
car mileage). In the second phase ( reduced subsample: participants who had held a driving
license for 10 years or less) , the predictors included were Gender, Age, Experience, Age ×
Experience, and the interaction terms involving Gender ( Gender × Age, Gender ×
Experience, and Gender × Age × Experience). Note that in this phase, the variables Year s
holding a driving license and Annual car mileage were replaced by a composite Experience
variable to assess driving experience. This variable was calculated by multiplying the number
of years holding a driving license by annual car mileage. We consider that this measure may
provide an assessment of driving experience that is closer to total lifetime mileage. I n the
first phase, potential fluctuations in annual mileage over time — particularly among
individuals w ith many years of driving experience — discouraged us from using this
co mposite measure . F or readers who may be interested, the results of the second phase using
Years holding a driving license and Annual car mileage as separate predictors are provided
in the supplementary material (see Section 2 ) .
When significant interactions were identified in the multiple regression analyses,
follow - up analyses using simple slopes were conducted to examine the pattern of the se
interactions. Specifically, the relationship between a given predictor and the criterion variable
was examined at the 16th (low), 50th ( medium ), and 84th (high) percentiles of the other
predictor.
All analyses were conducted using SPSS version 22
methods.endQuote: All analyses were conducted using SPSS version 22

associated with a reduced propensity for dangerous driving behavior, but only after sufficient
driv ing experience had been acquired. Furthermore, greater driving experience predicted

of age, possess lower driving skills due to their limited experience, which may encourage a
more cautious driving style, offsetting the greater risk - taking typicall y observed in youth.
However, once sufficient experience is gained, younger drivers demonstrate a marked

3.3. DDDI-NCED subscale

discussion.label: 4. Discussion
discussion.text:
discussion, t he present study aimed to take a step further in
understanding how age and driving experience are associated with dangerous driving
behavior. Rather than focusing solely on their individual associations or treating them as
covariates to be controlled for, we sought to examine the possibility of an interaction between
the se variables . This analysis was complemented by examining gender - related differences
and their potential moderating effect s on the relationship between age, experience, and
dangerous driving. We hypothesized the following: (H1) In terms of individual variables,
younger drivers will exhibit more dangerous driving behavior s than older drivers; with
respect to driving experience, however, no clear directional hypothesis can be formulated
regarding its relationship with dangerous driving behavior , given the mixed findings reported
in the literature. Nevertheless , we propose that (H2) the level of driving experience may
influence the relationship between age and dangerous behavior, as discussed above ; and (H3)
men will exhibit more dangerous driving behavior s than women. The i nteraction effects
involving gender , age , and driving experience will be explored.
To test these hypotheses, w e assessed drivers’ propensity for dangerous behavior
using the Dula Dangerous Driving Index ( DDDI ; risky driving and aggressive driving
subscales ) . Level s of driving experience w ere recorded based on the number of years holding
a driving license and annual car mileage. A nalyses were c onducted on two different samples:
the first comprised a broad community sample, while the second was restricted to a
subsample of drivers who had held a driving license for 10 years or less. This reduced
subsample was selected because the effects of experience are expected to be most pronounced
during the early stages of a driving career. Once sufficient experience is gained, changes in
driving behavior related to skill acquisition are less likely to occur. Moreover, this restriction
ensures a more balanced distribution of participants in terms of experience, preventing highly
experienced drivers from being concentrated exclusively among older individuals . Thus, b y
focusing on drivers in the initial years o f driving, the influence of experience and its potential
interaction with age may be examined more precisely .
quotes: 4. Discussion
5. Conclusion

conclusions.label: 5. Conclusion
conclusions.text:
5. Conclusion
This study provides a more comprehensive understanding of how age and driving
experience jointly relate to dangerous driving behavior, enabling clearer attribution of their
respective influences. Drawing on a large, community - based, gender - balanced sample of
drivers and using a recognized self - report measures of driving behavior (D DDI ), our findings
indicate that age and driving experience do not operate independently; rather, they interact in
shaping dangerous driving tendencies. Age did not provide a uniform protective effect; its
benefits became apparent only after drivers had acquired suffi cient experience, as novice
drivers may adopt a more cautious driving style due to their lack of driving skills and low
perceived self - efficacy. Furthermore, higher levels of driving experience were associated
with a greater frequency of dangerous behaviors, particularly among younger and
intermediate age groups. T hese associations were not always consistent across genders, with

distinct patterns observed for men and women. Overall, the findings of this research
underscore the importance of adopting multifactorial approaches to road safety that integrate
demographic, experiential, and contextual factors. They can have direct implications for
driver education, licensing policies, and prevention strategies, highlighting the need to
develop more refined road safety interventions that take into account the combined roles of
age, driving experience, and gender.

abstract.sample: While increasing age has been consistently associated with a safer driving style , the role of driving experie ... g contributing variables to guide the implementation of effective road - safety measures .
abstract.countedText:
While increasing age has been consistently associated with a safer driving style , the
role of driving experience and its interplay with age remain s insufficiently understood . Using
a large community - based sample of drivers, this study aimed to clarify the relative, joint, and
interactive contribution s of age, driving experience, and gender to the propensity to engage
in dangerous driving behaviors . The frequency of such behaviors w as assessed using the Dula
Dangerous Driving Index (DDDI). The sample included 1,882 drivers (50.9% men) with a
mean age of 38.11 years (range 18 – 79), a mean driving experience of 15.94 years, and a
mean annual mileage of 6,000 – 9,000 km. Results indicated that increasing age was
associated with a reduced propensity for dangerous driving behavior, but only after sufficient
driv ing experience had been acquired. Furthermore, greater driving experience predicted
higher dangerous driving behavior in younger and middle - aged drivers, while remaining
unrelated in older drivers. Notably, this pattern was more pronounced among men than
women. Th e observed interaction may be explained by the fact that novice drivers, regardless
of age, possess lower driving skills due to their limited experience, which may encourage a
more cautious driving style, offsetting the greater risk - taking typicall y observed in youth.
However, once sufficient experience is gained, younger drivers demonstrate a marked
tendency toward greater risk - taking behavior. These findings emphasize the need for
multifactorial approaches that account fo r the interaction s among contributing variables to
guide the implementation of effective road - safety measures .

How Age, Driving Experience, and Gender Interact in Predicting Dangerous Driving Behavior María Ángeles Ramos - Moreno 1 & Alberto Megías - Robles 1*
While increasing age has been consistently associated with a safer driving style , the role of driving experience and its interplay with age remain s insufficiently understood . Using a large community based sample of drivers, this study aimed to clarify the relative, joint, and interactive contribution s of age, driving experience, and gender to the propensity to engage in dangerous driving behaviors . The frequency of such behaviors w as assessed using the Dula Dangerous Driving Index (DDDI). The sample included 1,882 drivers (50.9% men) with a mean age of 38.11 years (range 18 – 79), a mean driving experience of 15.94 years, and a mean annual mileage of 6,000 – 9,000 km. Results indicated that increasing age was associated with a reduced propensity for dangerous driving behavior, but only after sufficient driv ing experience had been acquired. Furthermore, greater driving experience predicted higher dangerous driving behavior in younger and middle aged drivers, while remaining unrelated in older drivers. Notably, this pattern was more pronounced among men than women. Th e observed interaction may be explained by the fact that novice drivers, regardless of age, possess lower driving skills due to their limited experience, which may encourage a more cautious driving style, offsetting the greater risk taking typicall y observed in youth. However, once sufficient experience is gained, younger drivers demonstrate a marked tendency toward greater risk taking behavior. These findings emphasize the need for multifactorial approaches that account fo r the interaction s among contributing variables to guide the implementation of effective road safety measures .

results.title: An Individual Driving Behavior Portrait Approach for Professional Driver of HDVs with Naturalistic Driving Data.
results.abstract: More than 50% major road accidents are caused by risk driving behaviors from professional drivers of Heavy Duty Vehicles (HDVs). The quantitative estimation of driving performance and driving behaviors portrait for professional drivers is helpful to measure the driver's driving risk and inherent driving style. Previous studies have attempted to evaluate risk driving behavior, but most of them rely on high-demand vehicle and driving data. However, few studies can dig into the causes and correlations behind individual driving behavior and quantify the driving behaviors portrait for professional driver based on long-term naturalistic driving. In this study, the data is from On-Board Unit (OBU) devices mounted in the HDVs in China. Based on the driving behavior pattern diagram and the frequency and ranking of drivers' typical driving patterns, a driving behavior portrait approach is proposed by comprehensively considering the vehicle safety, driving comfort, and fuel economy indicators. The similarities and differences of different drivers' driving behaviors are quantitatively analyzed. The high precision and sampling frequency data from vehicles are used to verify the proposed approach. The results demonstrated that the driving behavior portrait approach can digitally describe the individual driving behaviors styles and identify the potential driving behaviors with long-term naturalistic driving data. The development of this approach can help quantitatively evaluate the individual characteristic of risk driving behaviors to prevent road accidents.

results.title: Age Predicts Older Adults' Driving Self-Regulation but Not Dangerous Driving Behaviors after Controlling for Executive Function.
results.abstract:
The aim of the study was to examine the unique contributions of age to objectively measure driving frequency and dangerous driving behaviors in healthy older adults after adjusting for executive function (EF).

A total of 28 community-dwelling older adults (mean age = 82.0 years, standard deviation [SD] = 7.5) without dementia who were in good physical health and enrolled in a longitudinal aging study completed several EF and clinical self-report measures at baseline. Participants subsequently had a sensor installed in their vehicle for a mean of 208 (SD = 38, range = 127-257) days.

Participants drove for an average of 54 min per day. Mixed-effects models indicated that after controlling for EF, older age was associated with less time driving per day, decreased number of trips, and less nighttime driving. Age was not associated with hard brakes or hard accelerations.

After accounting for EF, greater age is associated with higher driving self-regulation but not dangerous driving behaviors in healthy older adults. Future studies should recruit larger samples and collect sensor-measured driving data over a more extended time frame to better determine how and why these self-regulation changes take place.

results.title: Multi-scenario driving style research based on driving behavior pattern extraction.
results.abstract: Accurately analyzing drivers' driving styles is crucial for road safety and enhancing intelligent driving systems. However, existing studies have not fully explored the hidden information in driving sequences or considered the influence of driving environments on driving styles. Based on natural driving data from electric vehicles in Wuhan, a framework for driving style analysis based on driving behavior pattern extraction was proposed. Driving sequences were extracted under free-driving and car-following scenarios, where the convergence of driving features was verified using kernel density estimation and relative entropy. A driving propensity indicator based on a dynamic threshold was constructed, and combined with the Hierarchical Dirichlet Process Hidden Semi-Markov Model (HDP-HSMM) and K-means clustering algorithm, 4 and 5 types of driving behavior pattern were extracted under free-driving and car-following scenarios, respectively. Energy consumption distribution was introduced to verify the validity of driving pattern extraction. Jensen-Shannon (JS) divergence was utilized to calculate the difference in the distribution of the driving propensity indicator among different drivers. By quantifying behavioral differences, drivers were categorized into aggressive, moderate, and conservative types. The results show that the statistical characteristics of driving patterns are consistent with the distribution of energy consumption, with the highest energy consumption occurs in aggressive acceleration and high-speed steady-state patterns, and the highest braking energy recovery occurs in aggressive deceleration pattern. Furthermore, the driving environment influences driving styles to certain degree while exhibiting consistent or diverse driving styles in different driving scenarios and patterns.

results.title: Age-related differences in driving behaviors among non-professional drivers in Egypt.
results.abstract:
This study aimed to investigate whether young and old non-professional drivers in South Egypt have aberrant driving behaviors compared with their middle-aged counterparts.

In this cross-sectional study, a total of 1764 non-professional drivers aged ≥ 19 years, residing in Beni-Suef in South Egypt, and having ≥ one year of driving experience were randomly selected. All drivers were asked to fill out a self-administered questionnaire, including personal information, driving habits, and the Arabic version of the Driver Behavior Questionnaire which evaluates driving violations, errors, and lapses.

This study included 560 young drivers (19-25 years), 850 middle-aged drivers (26-59 years), and 354 old drivers (≥ 60 years). Compared with middle-aged drivers, young drivers reported more non-use of the seatbelt, eating while driving, and driving while feeling drowsy. Old drivers, in contrast, showed more careful driving behaviors including fewer violations, errors, and lapses and less likelihood of driving while feeling sleepy.

This study supports the conception that young drivers pose less careful driving habits. Initiating educational programs targeting young drivers to improve their driving habits and create a traffic safety culture in Egypt is highly warranted.

results.title: The Influence of Personality and Demographic Characteristics on Aggressive Driving Behaviors in Eastern Chinese Drivers.
results.abstract:
Road safety research is important due to the large number of road traffic fatalities globally. This study investigated the influences of age, driving experience and other covariates on aggressive driving behavior.

A cross-sectional survey was conducted in Yixing City, Wuxi City, Jiangsu Province, China. Regression analysis was applied to explore the influences of age and driving experience and their interactions with other covariates on aggressive driving behavior. Two analyses methodologies were used to assess the simple effect of the interactions. Firstly, the Jamovi automatic analysis classification program was used to calculate the simple slope test. Second, the SPSS macro program was also used to calculate the simple slope test also.

A total of 570 drivers (247 males, 282 females) participated in the survey. A negative correlation was found between age and aggressive driving behaviors, and a positive correlation was found between neuroticism and aggressive driving behaviors in the multiple regression analysis. Significant associations were also found between age, driving experience, and depression, as well as age, driving experience, and neuroticism. Simple slope tests showed that depressive symptoms could increase aggressive behaviors in the elderly and experienced drivers. When experiencing neuroticism, individuals with higher driving experience were more aggressive in driving than shorter experienced drivers.

Age and neuroticism influenced aggressive driving behaviors. Veteran drivers could be aggressive drivers when experiencing depressive symptoms or neuroticism. Mobile intervention could be sent to the potentially risky drivers, which would be safe and broadly feasible to prevent aggressive driving behavior in the background of COVID-19.

results.title: An exploration of the role of driving experience on self-reported and real-world aberrant driving behaviors.
results.abstract: A significant proportion of global road crashes are attributed to unsafe driving behaviors. The current study aimed to explore potential differences in driving behaviors across experienced and novice drivers using two separate approaches; a questionnaire study and an instrumented vehicle study (IVS). The analysis of 260 questionnaires and 1,372 traffic interactions within the IVS revelated that driving experience affects driving performance for different driving tasks. Factor analysis of the questionnaire data revealed the impact of driving errors, lapses, violations, and aggressive violations on the behavior of novice and experienced drivers. Behavioral models of novice and experienced drivers encountering other road users were determined using binary logistic regression. The results showed that novice drivers were more likely to engage in driving violations while experienced drivers were more likely to engage in aggressive violations. Unauthorized speeding, zigzag movements, using a mobile phone while driving, and unauthorized overtaking on roads were the most frequent driving violations by novice drivers. The most frequent aggressive violations by experienced drivers were tempting other drivers to create a race and chasing other drivers. These findings may be used as a framework to facilitate safer driving behaviors by reducing errors, lapses, violations and aggressive violations, and facilitating safety-promoting attitudes.

results.title: Risky driving behavior among individuals with Autism, ADHD, and typically developing persons.
results.abstract:
Many individuals with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) and Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) often experience difficulty with driving, including difficulty with obtaining a driver's license as well as driving safely and efficiently. Such difficulties negatively impact their ability to function independently and participate in daily activities that require driving. ASD and ADHD commonly occur co-morbidly and share many overlapping clinical features. Few studies have directly compared the nature of difficulties in driving safety outcomes between ASD and ADHD. The overarching goal of the current study was to characterize and compare self-reported driving behavior among young autistic drivers, ADHD drivers, and typically developing (TD) drivers.

Fifty-four participants (14 ASD, 20 ADHD, 20 TD); ages 16-30) completed the Autism Spectrum Quotient and ADHD Adult Rating scale as a method of screening of symptoms. All three groups then completed the Driving Behavior Questionnaire (DBQ), which measured self-reported driving violations, driving errors, and overall risky driving behavior. The three groups of ASD, ADHD, and TD individuals were then compared regarding symptomology and driving behavior differences.

One-way ANOVAs indicated group differences in DBQ total scores and DBQ errors. Drivers with ADHD reported significantly greater overall risky driving behaviors and driving errors compared to ASD and TD drivers. There were no significant differences between ASD and TD drivers in reported risky driving behaviors and errors. Linear regressions indicated that among all drivers, self-reported ADHD symptoms were significantly associated with higher levels of self-reported overall risky driving and driving errors, regardless of diagnostic group.

Risky driving and driving errors may be more closely related to symptoms that are characteristic of ADHD. This has implications for individuals with ADHD and autistic individuals who often show or report higher rates of ADHD symptoms. Future studies should compare driving skills of ASD and ADHD drivers using objective measures of driving performance, such as driving simulators or on-road tests.

results.title: Driving Behavior Based Relative Risk Evaluation Using a Nonparametric Optimization Method.
results.journal: International journal of environmental research and public health
results.abstract: Evaluating risks when driving is a valuable method by which to make people better understand their driving behavior, and also provides the basis for improving driving performance. In many existing risk evaluation studies, however, most of the time only the occurrence frequency of risky driving events is considered in the time dimension and fixed weights allocation is adopted when constructing a risk evaluation model. In this study, we develop a driving behavior-based relative risk evaluation model using a nonparametric optimization method, in which both the frequency and the severity level of different risky driving behaviors are taken into account, and the concept of relative risk instead of absolute risk is proposed. In the case study, based on the data from a naturalistic driving experiment, various risky driving behaviors are identified, and the proposed model is applied to assess the overall risk related to the distance travelled by an individual driver during a specific driving segment, relative to other drivers on other segments, and it is further compared with an absolute risk evaluation. The results show that the proposed model is superior in avoiding the absolute risk quantification of all kinds of risky driving behaviors, and meanwhile, a prior knowledge on the contribution of different risky driving behaviors to the overall risk is not required. Such a model has a wide range of application scenarios, and is valuable for feedback research relating to safe driving, for a personalized insurance assessment based on drivers' behavior, and for the safety evaluation of professional drivers such as ride-hailing drivers.

results.title: Driving Style Recognition Based on Electroencephalography Data From a Simulated Driving Experiment.
results.abstract: Driving style is a very important indicator and a crucial measurement of a driver's performance and ability to drive in a safe and protective manner. A dangerous driving style would possibly result in dangerous behaviors. If the driving styles can be recognized by some appropriate classification methods, much attention could be paid to the drivers with dangerous driving styles. The driving style recognition module can be integrated into the advanced driving assistance system (ADAS), which integrates different modules to improve driving automation, safety and comfort, and then the driving safety could be enhanced by pre-warning the drivers or adjusting the vehicle's controlling parameters when the dangerous driving style is detected. In most previous studies, driver's questionnaire data and vehicle's objective driving data were utilized to recognize driving styles. And promising results were obtained. However, these methods were indirect or subjective in driving style evaluation. In this paper a method based on objective driving data and electroencephalography (EEG) data was presented to classify driving styles. A simulated driving system was constructed and the EEG data and the objective driving data were collected synchronously during the simulated driving. The driving style of each participant was classified by clustering the driving data via K-means. Then the EEG data was denoised and the amplitude and the Power Spectral Density (PSD) of four frequency bands were extracted as the EEG features by Fast Fourier transform and Welch. Finally, the EEG features, combined with the classification results of the driving data were used to train a Support Vector Machine (SVM) model and a leave-one-subject-out cross validation was utilized to evaluate the performance. The SVM classification accuracy was about 80.0%. Conservative drivers showed higher PSDs in the parietal and occipital areas in the alpha and beta bands, aggressive drivers showed higher PSD in the temporal area in the delta and theta bands. These results imply that different driving styles were related with different driving strategies and mental states and suggest the feasibility of driving style recognition from EEG patterns.

results.title: Executive function and dangerous driving behaviors in young drivers.
results.journal: Transportation research. Part F, Traffic psychology and behaviour
results.abstract: The purpose of the present study was to investigate the behavioral and cognitive processes underlying dangerous driving behaviors. We used a survey to assess levels of executive function in college students. The sample consisted of 59 males and 77 females and their age ranged from 18 to 24. We stratified the students into two groups based on executive function scores and compared the extent to which each group engaged in four dangerous driving behaviors (texting while driving, driving without a seat belt, driving while intoxicated, and speeding) as well as how often they experienced three negative driving outcomes (crashes, pulled over, and ticketed). We also investigated how these driving behaviors and outcomes are correlated with subcategories of executive function. The results show that students with a low level of executive function were more likely to engage in dangerous driving behaviors and more likely to experience negative driving outcomes. The results also show that texting while driving, driving while intoxicated, and speeding were most strongly correlated with the executive function subcategory of Impulse Control, whereas driving without a seat belt was most strongly correlated with the executive function subcategory of Strategic Planning. These results suggest that different behavioral or cognitive processes are involved in different dangerous driving behaviors and different interventions may be needed to target each underlying process.

These findings emphasize the need for multifactorial approaches that account fo r the interaction s among contributing variables to guide the implementation of effective road safety measures .
